title: Book spine reback
subtitle: Relining a failed spine without disbinding the text block
excerpt: When the spine of a casebound book splits from its case but the sewing is still sound, the repair is a reback: the old covering is peeled back at the spine, the hinge is cleaned, a new cloth strip is glued in, and the covering is refitted. The book is usable again without a full rebind.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on rebacking and restoration. Internet Archive scan available. Techniques described here are consistent with Cockerell's repair procedures.

glossaryTerms.definition: The narrow flexible zone between the spine and the front or back board of a casebound book. When the hinge fails, the board detaches.
A repair that replaces the spine lining and joint of a casebound book without disbinding the text block.
A layer of mull or super cloth glued across the spine of a sewn text block to reinforce the sewing and hold the case in place.

content.text: Most casebound books fail at the
hinge
rather than at the sewing. The cloth turns brittle; the adhesive at the joint dries out; the board pivots away from the spine. If the sewing is still intact, a
reback
restores the book without the time and risk of a full rebind.
Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Keep the non-cutting hand behind the blade's path when paring old spine linings away.
What you need
New cloth: book cloth cut to the height of the spine plus 20 mm extra at head and tail, and wide enough to overlap onto both boards by 20 mm.
Mull: a strip of book mull or super cloth cut to the same height as the text block and wide enough to overlap each board by 20 mm.
Adhesive: acid-free PVA, medium consistency. A watered-down methylated spirit solution (1:3) is used to soften old adhesive before removal.
Preparing the spine
Carefully peel the endpapers back from the inside of the boards with a bone folder and damp sponge if they are stuck. Do not tear. The endpapers will be re-pasted at the end.
Separate the text block from the case by working the craft knife gently along the old spine joint. The old
spine lining
may lift away; if it is still sound, leave it in place.
Remove old adhesive from the spine and joint channels with a dampened cotton pad. Allow the spine to dry fully before proceeding.
Applying the new lining and cloth
Apply a thin coat of PVA to the spine. Lay the mull strip centred on the spine and press flat. Allow to tack.

Lay the book with the spine horizontal. Apply PVA to the mull wings and press each board closed onto the mull, aligning the board edges carefully. Clamp lightly with the book press while the mull sets (about 20 minutes).

Apply PVA to the new book-cloth strip on its reverse face. Lay it centred on the spine, overlapping each board by 20 mm. Work out any air from the centre with the bone folder. Fold the excess cloth over the head and tail of the spine and cut the corners before turning in.
Re-paste the endpapers onto the inside boards to cover the mull wings and the cloth turn-ins. Clamp immediately in the book press and leave overnight.
Troubleshooting

attrs.cause: The mull wings were not pressed firmly enough into the joint channel before the cloth went on.

content.text: Work fresh PVA into the joint channel with a thin stiff brush, close the board, and re-clamp overnight.

attrs.symptom: New cloth pulls away at the spine head
attrs.cause: Cloth turn-ins were not mitred or pressed firmly; the excess bulk at the corners lifted as the adhesive dried.

content.text: Glue the lifted turn-in flat with fresh PVA, snip the corner to remove bulk, and clamp with small binder clips padded with waste card.
Care
A rebacked book will outlast the original repair if stored upright between books of similar height. Avoid stacking heavy items on top of a freshly rebacked book for the first week.
